# Extractor Receipt

## Summary

This source contains benchmark construction details, comparative performance results for coding agents with and without repository context files, cost and behavior trace findings, and limitations about when context files may help.

## Evidence Files

### file: evidence-01.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-03-001
evidence_id: evidence-01
kind: metric
citation: Gloaguen et al., 2026, Section 3
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.11988
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
AGENTbench contains 138 task instances drawn from 12 repositories that include developer-written context files.

## Source Excerpt

“The benchmark contains 138 138 unique instances, covering both bug-fixing and feature addition tasks across 12 12 recent and niche repositories, which all feature developer-written context files.”

## Note

This benchmark is specialized to repositories that already adopted context files.
```

### file: evidence-02.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-03-001
evidence_id: evidence-02
kind: result
citation: Gloaguen et al., 2026, Section 4.2
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.11988
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
LLM-generated context files reduced average resolution rate by 0.5% on SWE-bench Lite and 2% on AGENTbench relative to no context files.

## Source Excerpt

“LLM-generated context files cause performance drops in 5 out of 8 settings across SWE-bench Lite and AGENTbench... In more detail, the average resolution rate is reduced by 0.5 % 0.5\% and 2 % 2\% on average on SWE-bench Lite and AGENTbench , respectively.”

## Note

The reported effect is an average across evaluated agents and benchmarks.
```

### file: evidence-03.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-03-001
evidence_id: evidence-03
kind: metric
citation: Gloaguen et al., 2026, Section 4.2
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.11988
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
LLM-generated context files increased agent effort, adding 2.45 average steps on SWE-bench Lite and 3.92 on AGENTbench, with average cost increases of 20% and 23%.

## Source Excerpt

“Meanwhile, the context files increase the # steps in every setting on average by 2.45 2.45 and 3.92 3.92 steps, respectively, which leads to a cost increase of 20% and 23% on average, respectively.”

## Note

The source reports these as average changes across settings rather than per-task guarantees.
```

### file: evidence-04.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-03-001
evidence_id: evidence-04
kind: result
citation: Gloaguen et al., 2026, Section 4.2
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.11988
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
On AGENTbench, developer-provided context files outperformed LLM-generated files for all four agents and improved performance over no context files for all agents except Claude Code.

## Source Excerpt

“We observe that the developer-provided context files outperform the LLM-generated ones for all four agents, despite not being agent-specific, and improve the performance compared to no context files for all agents but Claude Code.”

## Note

This performance gain is not uniform across all agents because Claude Code is the stated exception.
```

### file: evidence-05.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-03-001
evidence_id: evidence-05
kind: metric
citation: Gloaguen et al., 2026, Section 4.2
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.11988
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
Developer-provided context files also increased effort, adding 3.34 average steps and raising costs by as much as 19%.

## Source Excerpt

“However, developer-provided context files also increase the average number of steps and costs required to solve the task, on average by 3.34 3.34 steps and at most 19%, respectively.”

## Note

This evidence pairs a reported performance benefit with higher execution cost.
```

### file: evidence-06.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-03-001
evidence_id: evidence-06
kind: result
citation: Gloaguen et al., 2026, Section 4.2
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.11988
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Context files did not meaningfully reduce the number of steps before agents first interacted with files modified in the original PR patch.

## Source Excerpt

“To assess the usefulness of these overviews, we measure how quickly agents discover files relevant to the described issue... Both on SWE-bench Lite and AGENTbench the presence of context files does not meaningfully reduce this metric, as shown in Figure ˜ 4.”

## Note

This evidence bears on the claimed repository-overview function of context files by showing little measured improvement in reaching relevant files.
```

### file: evidence-07.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-03-001
evidence_id: evidence-07
kind: result
citation: Gloaguen et al., 2026, Section 4.2
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.11988
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
When other documentation was removed after context-file generation, LLM-generated context files improved performance by 2.7% on average and outperformed developer-written documentation.

## Source Excerpt

“In this setting, where context files are the only source of documentation available, LLM-generated context files not only consistently improve performance by 2.7 2.7 % on average, but also outperform developer-written documentation.”

## Note

This result comes from an ablation where documentation files were manually removed, so it is a different setting from the main benchmark comparison.
```

### file: evidence-08.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-03-001
evidence_id: evidence-08
kind: result
citation: Gloaguen et al., 2026, Section 4.3
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.11988
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
With context files present, coding agents ran more tests across all models.

## Source Excerpt

“We find that, across all models, when context files are present, the coding agents run more tests.”

## Note

The source presents this as a trace-analysis result on tool-use frequency.
```

### file: evidence-09.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-03-001
evidence_id: evidence-09
kind: result
citation: Gloaguen et al., 2026, Section 4.3
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.11988
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
With context files present, coding agents searched more files, read more files, wrote more files, and used more repository-specific tooling.

## Source Excerpt

“They also tend to navigate the repository more: they search more files ( grep ), read more files, and write more files. Lastly, adding context files causes agents to use more repository-specific tooling (e.g., uv and repo_tool ).”

## Note

The reported increases are behavioral trace findings, not direct measures of task success.
```

### file: evidence-10.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-03-001
evidence_id: evidence-10
kind: metric
citation: Gloaguen et al., 2026, Section 4.3
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.11988
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Agents used named tools far more often when those tools were mentioned in context files: uv averaged 1.6 uses per instance when mentioned versus under 0.01 when not mentioned, and repository-specific tools averaged 2.5 versus under 0.05.

## Source Excerpt

“For instance, uv is used 1.6 times per instance on average when mentioned in the context files, compared to fewer than 0.01 times when it is not mentioned, and repository-specific tools are used 2.5 times per instance on average when mentioned, compared to fewer than 0.05 times when they are not mentioned.”

## Note

This evidence supports instruction-following behavior rather than improved task outcomes.
```

### file: evidence-11.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-03-001
evidence_id: evidence-11
kind: metric
citation: Gloaguen et al., 2026, Section 4.3
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.11988
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Context files increased average reasoning-token use for GPT-5.2 and GPT-5.1 mini: on SWE-bench Lite by 22% and 14% for LLM-generated files, and on AGENTbench by 14% and 10%; developer-written files increased reasoning tokens by 20% and 2% on AGENTbench.

## Source Excerpt

“In Figure ˜ 7 , we show that LLM-generated context files indeed increase the average number of reasoning tokens by 22% for GPT-5.2 and 14% for GPT-5.1 mini on SWE-bench Lite (respectively 14% and 10% on AGENTbench ), and that developer-written context files increase the number of reasoning tokens by 20% and 2% for GPT-5.2 and GPT-5.1 mini , respectively.”

## Note

The source reports reasoning-token effects only for GPT-5.2 and GPT-5.1 mini because those models expose adaptive reasoning behavior.
---

Context files increased average reasoning-token use for GPT-5.2 and GPT-5.1 mini: on SWE-bench Lite by 22% and 14% for LLM-generated files, and on AGENTbench by 14% and 10%; developer-written files increased reasoning tokens by 20% and 2% on AGENTbench.

## Source Excerpt

“In Figure ˜ 7 , we show that LLM-generated context files indeed increase the average number of reasoning tokens by 22% for GPT-5.2 and 14% for GPT-5.1 mini on SWE-bench Lite (respectively 14% and 10% on AGENTbench ), and that developer-written context files increase the number of reasoning tokens by 20% and 2% for GPT-5.2 and GPT-5.1 mini , respectively.”

## Note

The source reports reasoning-token effects only for GPT-5.2 and GPT-5.1 mini because those models expose adaptive reasoning behavior.

# Extractor Receipt

## Summary

This source contains benchmark construction details, comparative performance results for coding agents with and without repository context files, cost and behavior trace findings, and limitations about when context files may help.

## Evidence Files

AGENTbench contains 138 task instances drawn from 12 repositories that include developer-written context files.

## Source Excerpt

“The benchmark contains 138 138 unique instances, covering both bug-fixing and feature addition tasks across 12 12 recent and niche repositories, which all feature developer-written context files.”

## Note

This benchmark is specialized to repositories that already adopted context files.
```

### file: evidence-02.md
```markdown
---

---
LLM-generated context files reduced average resolution rate by 0.5% on SWE-bench Lite and 2% on AGENTbench relative to no context files.

## Source Excerpt

“LLM-generated context files cause performance drops in 5 out of 8 settings across SWE-bench Lite and AGENTbench... In more detail, the average resolution rate is reduced by 0.5 % 0.5\% and 2 % 2\% on average on SWE-bench Lite and AGENTbench , respectively.”

## Note

The reported effect is an average across evaluated agents and benchmarks.
```

### file: evidence-03.md
```markdown
---

contradiction_relevance: none
---
LLM-generated context files increased agent effort, adding 2.45 average steps on SWE-bench Lite and 3.92 on AGENTbench, with average cost increases of 20% and 23%.

## Source Excerpt

“Meanwhile, the context files increase the # steps in every setting on average by 2.45 2.45 and 3.92 3.92 steps, respectively, which leads to a cost increase of 20% and 23% on average, respectively.”

## Note

The source reports these as average changes across settings rather than per-task guarantees.
```

### file: evidence-04.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-03-001
evidence_id: evidence-04
kind: result
citation: Gloaguen et al., 2026, Section 4.2
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.11988
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
On AGENTbench, developer-provided context files outperformed LLM-generated files for all four agents and improved performance over no context files for all agents except Claude Code.

## Source Excerpt

“We observe that the developer-provided context files outperform the LLM-generated ones for all four agents, despite not being agent-specific, and improve the performance compared to no context files for all agents but Claude Code.”

## Note

This performance gain is not uniform across all agents because Claude Code is the stated exception.
```

```markdown
---

Developer-provided context files also increased effort, adding 3.34 average steps and raising costs by as much as 19%.

## Source Excerpt

“However, developer-provided context files also increase the average number of steps and costs required to solve the task, on average by 3.34 3.34 steps and at most 19%, respectively.”

## Note

This evidence pairs a reported performance benefit with higher execution cost.
```

---

Context files did not meaningfully reduce the number of steps before agents first interacted with files modified in the original PR patch.

## Source Excerpt

“To assess the usefulness of these overviews, we measure how quickly agents discover files relevant to the described issue... Both on SWE-bench Lite and AGENTbench the presence of context files does not meaningfully reduce this metric, as shown in Figure ˜ 4.”

## Note

This evidence bears on the claimed repository-overview function of context files by showing little measured improvement in reaching relevant files.
```

### file: evidence-07.md

---

contradiction_relevance: tension
---
When other documentation was removed after context-file generation, LLM-generated context files improved performance by 2.7% on average and outperformed developer-written documentation.

## Source Excerpt

“In this setting, where context files are the only source of documentation available, LLM-generated context files not only consistently improve performance by 2.7 2.7 % on average, but also outperform developer-written documentation.”

## Note

This result comes from an ablation where documentation files were manually removed, so it is a different setting from the main benchmark comparison.
```

### file: evidence-09.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-03-001
evidence_id: evidence-09

source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.11988
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
With context files present, coding agents searched more files, read more files, wrote more files, and used more repository-specific tooling.

## Source Excerpt

“They also tend to navigate the repository more: they search more files ( grep ), read more files, and write more files. Lastly, adding context files causes agents to use more repository-specific tooling (e.g., uv and repo_tool ).”

## Note

The reported increases are behavioral trace findings, not direct measures of task success.
```

### file: evidence-10.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-03-001
evidence_id: evidence-10
kind: metric
citation: Gloaguen et al., 2026, Section 4.3
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.11988
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Agents used named tools far more often when those tools were mentioned in context files: uv averaged 1.6 uses per instance when mentioned versus under 0.01 when not mentioned, and repository-specific tools averaged 2.5 versus under 0.05.

## Source Excerpt

“For instance, uv is used 1.6 times per instance on average when mentioned in the context files, compared to fewer than 0.01 times when it is not mentioned, and repository-specific tools are used 2.5 times per instance on average when mentioned, compared to fewer than 0.05 times when they are not mentioned.”

## Note

This evidence supports instruction-following behavior rather than improved task outcomes.
```
